questions.stem: A 5-week-old male presents with a 3-day history of progressively projectile, non-bilious vomiting after every feed. He is hungry immediately after vomiting. On examination, visible peristaltic waves are seen from left to right across the epigastrium, and a firm, olive-shaped mass is palpated in the right upper quadrant. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Projectile non-bilious vomiting with visible peristalsis and a palpable olive-shaped mass is pathognomonic for pyloric stenosis. Gastro-oesophageal reflux typically causes non-projectile vomiting and is not associated with an abdominal mass or visible peristalsis.

questions.stem: A 10-week-old female presents with a 2-week history of gradually worsening vomiting, now projectile and non-bilious. She has been treated for gastro-oesophageal reflux with thickened feeds and positioning but has not improved. Weight gain has slowed. Abdominal examination reveals a palpable olive-shaped mass. Electrolytes show hypokalaemic, hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Although pyloric stenosis often presents before 8 weeks, it can occur later. The classic findings of projectile vomiting, palpable olive mass, and metabolic alkalosis confirm the diagnosis despite the atypical age. Pylorospasm is intermittent and does not cause persistent alkalosis or an olive mass.

questions.stem: A 4-week-old male presents to the emergency department with severe lethargy, sunken eyes, and dry mucous membranes. The parents report forceful vomiting after every feed for the past week, which has become projectile over the last 2 days. He has passed only one wet nappy in the last 24 hours. Blood gases show a pH of 7.52, bicarbonate 32 mmol/L, PCO2 6.0 kPa, and a chloride of 88 mmol/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Septic shock due to urinary tract infection
questions.explanation: Hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis in an infant with projectile vomiting is characteristic of pyloric stenosis, even when the presentation is dominated by dehydration and shock. The other options do not typically produce this electrolyte pattern with projectile vomiting.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old female is brought in with a 1-week history of non-bilious vomiting after most feeds. Her mother describes the vomiting as occasionally forceful but not always. She has been diagnosed with gastro-oesophageal reflux and is on omeprazole, but her weight is static. Abdominal examination shows mild epigastric distension but no palpable mass. An upper gastrointestinal contrast study reveals a narrow, elongated pyloric canal and a double-track sign. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The double-track sign on upper GI contrast study is characteristic of pyloric stenosis even if the olive is not palpable. Non-bilious vomiting with failure to thrive despite anti-reflux treatment also points to this diagnosis. Pylorospasm would not show a fixed narrowed canal.

questions.stem: A 3-week-old male, born at term, presents with a 4-day history of projectile, non-bilious vomiting after each feed. His mother reports that his older brother had a similar condition and underwent a surgical procedure as an infant. On examination, the infant appears hungry and has visible peristalsis. No abdominal mass is palpated. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A positive family history of pyloric stenosis in combination with projectile vomiting and visible peristalsis makes pyloric stenosis the most likely diagnosis even without a palpable olive. The absence of a mass does not exclude it, especially early. Gastro-oesophageal reflux is not typically projectile and familial.

questions.stem: A 4-week-old male presents with projectile non-bilious vomiting after every feed. Weight is falling. Examination reveals a palpable olive-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is the first-line, non-invasive investigation. It visualises the hypertrophied pylorus (muscle thickness >3 mm, length >14 mm). Upper GI barium study is reserved if ultrasound is equivocal. CT and endoscopy are not indicated.

questions.stem: A 5-week-old infant presents with persistent vomiting and weight loss. Vomiting is non-bilious but not clearly projectile. Electrolytes show hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis (Na 135, K 3.2, Cl 92, HCO3 32). Abdominal ultrasound is inconclusive. What is the best next investigation?
questions.explanation: When ultrasound is equivocal, an upper GI contrast study is next. It demonstrates the 'string sign' or 'double track' of the narrowed pyloric channel. CT and barium enema are not appropriate. Repeating ultrasound delays diagnosis.

questions.stem: During an abdominal ultrasound for suspected pyloric stenosis, which of the following measurements is most consistent with the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The diagnostic threshold for pyloric stenosis is muscle thickness >3 mm (typically 3-4 mm) and length >14 mm. Options A and B are normal. Option D is too short. Pyloric diameter is not a standard criterion.

questions.stem: A 3-week-old infant presents with bilious vomiting. Which investigation is most appropriate to distinguish possible malrotation from pyloric stenosis?
questions.explanation: Bilious vomiting suggests obstruction distal to the ampulla of Vater. An upper GI contrast study can identify malrotation (abnormal duodenojejunal junction) or if non-bilious, the string sign of pyloric stenosis. Abdominal ultrasound is not reliable for malrotation.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old infant with projectile vomiting has the following venous blood gas: pH 7.50, pCO2 5.6 kPa, HCO3- 32 mmol/L, base excess +8. Which electrolyte abnormality is most characteristically associated with this condition?
questions.explanation: Persistent vomiting of gastric contents leads to loss of hydrochloric acid, causing a hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis. Hypochloraemia is the hallmark electrolyte disturbance. Other options are not typical of pyloric stenosis.

questions.stem: A 4-week-old infant presents with projectile vomiting. An ultrasound confirms hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. Blood gas shows hypokalaemic hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis. Which of the following medications is contraindicated in the initial management of this infant?
questions.explanation: Metoclopramide is contraindicated due to risk of extrapyramidal side effects and lack of efficacy in pyloric stenosis. Standard management includes IV fluid correction and NGT decompression, not prokinetics.

questions.stem: A 5-week-old infant with confirmed pyloric stenosis is severely dehydrated, with a capillary refill time of 4 seconds and sunken eyes. Serum electrolytes show Na 132, K 3.0, Cl 88, HCO3 32. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial intravenous fluid regimen?
options: 0.45% sodium chloride with 5% dextrose and 20 mmol/L potassium chloride
0.9% sodium chloride with 20 mmol/L potassium chloride
questions.explanation: The correct choice replaces ongoing losses with a hypotonic solution (0.45% NaCl) and adds KCl to correct hypokalaemia once urine output is established. Option C is hypotonic and contraindicated; options A, E lack dextrose; D is closer but B is standard.

questions.stem: A 3-week-old infant undergoes successful pyloromyotomy. On the first postoperative day, the infant has one episode of non-projectile vomiting. The abdomen is soft and non-distended. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: Postoperative vomiting is common due to gastric atony and usually resolves spontaneously. Reassurance is appropriate. Further imaging is reserved for persistent or bilious vomiting.

questions.stem: A 2-week-old preterm neonate (born at 32 weeks) is diagnosed with pyloric stenosis. Which of the following intravenous fluids is contraindicated for preoperative resuscitation?
questions.explanation: 0.18% sodium chloride with 4% dextrose is hypotonic and can cause hyponatraemia, especially in neonates with metabolic alkalosis; it is contraindicated. Isotonic or near-isotonic fluids are preferred.

questions.stem: A 4-week-old infant undergoes an uncomplicated pyloromyotomy. When should oral feeds be initiated postoperatively?
questions.explanation: Standard practice is to start small feeds (e.g., 10–15 ml) around 6 hours post-surgery and gradually increase. Immediate feeding risks vomiting; prolonged delay is unnecessary.

questions.stem: A 3-week-old boy presents with projectile vomiting after every feed for 3 days. He is hungry immediately after vomiting. On examination, visible peristalsis is seen in the upper abdomen, but no palpable olive is felt. He is well-hydrated with normal fontanelle. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Admit for IV fluids and observation
questions.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is the first-line investigation for suspected pyloric stenosis. A barium meal is no longer routinely used due to radiation exposure. Surgical referral is premature without confirmation, and ranitidine is not indicated. Admission and IV fluids are not needed as the baby is well-hydrated.

questions.stem: A 4-week-old boy presents with projectile vomiting, lethargy, sunken fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, and tachypnoea. Capillary refill is 4 seconds. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: The baby has signs of severe dehydration and shock. Immediate IV fluid resuscitation with a bolus of 0.9% saline is the priority. Ultrasound or surgical referral can wait until haemodynamically stable. NG tube is not indicated initially. Capillary glucose should be checked during fluid resuscitation but the bolus is the single most important first action.

questions.stem: A 5-week-old with confirmed pyloric stenosis is awaiting surgery after initial fluid resuscitation. Current electrolytes: Na 138, K 2.8, Cl 88, HCO3 35. She is still having nasogastric losses. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Start IV 0.45% saline + 5% dextrose + 20 mmol KCl per 500 mL
questions.explanation: Hypokalaemia and hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis are typical. Before surgery, potassium must be replaced. IV fluid with potassium is the safest route as the baby is nil by mouth. Oral potassium is not appropriate. Surgery is delayed until electrolytes are corrected. Repeating without action is insufficient.

questions.stem: A 2-week-old ex-34-week preterm infant presents with non-bilious vomiting over 3 days, worse after feeds. She is hungry but vomits shortly after. Abdominal examination shows no palpable olive. Abdominal ultrasound reports pyloric muscle thickness of 3.5 mm (borderline, normal <3 mm in term infants; preterm thresholds are lower). She is well-hydrated. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Start a trial of anti-reflux thickened feeds
Arrange an upper GI contrast study
questions.explanation: With a borderline ultrasound in a preterm infant, an upper GI contrast study can confirm or exclude pyloric stenosis by identifying the 'string sign'. A repeat ultrasound may delay diagnosis. Anti-reflux measures are unlikely to help. Surgical referral requires a definitive diagnosis. Discharge is unsafe given the clinical suspicion.

questions.stem: A 4-week-old infant presents with a 3-day history of progressive projectile, non-bilious vomiting after feeds. The parents report the vomiting is becoming more forceful. Which single finding in this infant is a red flag that mandates immediate exclusion of a life-threatening surgical condition?
options: Visible gastric peristalsis from left to right
Palpable olive-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant
questions.explanation: Bilious vomiting in a young infant indicates potential malrotation with midgut volvulus, a surgical emergency requiring urgent imaging and intervention. In contrast, non-bilious projectile vomiting with an olive mass is classic for pyloric stenosis, which is not immediately life-threatening.

questions.stem: A 5-week-old infant with confirmed pyloric stenosis is awaiting pyloromyotomy. He is being managed with intravenous fluids and nasogastric drainage. Which laboratory finding in this infant is a red flag that indicates severe metabolic decompensation and requires urgent correction before surgery?
questions.explanation: A serum bicarbonate >30 mmol/L indicates severe metabolic alkalosis, which is a common threshold for urgent intravenous fluid and electrolyte correction prior to anaesthesia. While hypokalemia and hypochloremia are also present, the elevated bicarbonate reflects the severity of the alkalosis and risk of complications.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old infant with undiagnosed pyloric stenosis presents to the emergency department. On examination, the infant is lethargic, with sunken eyes, reduced skin turgor, and dry mucous membranes. Which of the following electrolyte disturbances is most likely to be life-threatening in this infant?
questions.explanation: Hypokalemia in pyloric stenosis results from gastric losses and metabolic alkalosis. Severe hypokalemia can cause cardiac arrhythmias and muscle weakness, posing an immediate risk. Other electrolyte disturbances are less characteristic of this condition.

questions.stem: A 4-week-old infant with known pyloric stenosis develops a sudden tonic-clonic seizure. Which of the following is the most likely life-threatening complication causing this presentation?
questions.explanation: Severe metabolic alkalosis in pyloric stenosis can cause decreased ionized calcium, leading to tetany and seizures. While hypoglycaemia can occur, the classic seizure complication in this setting is hypocalcaemia secondary to alkalosis. This is a can't-miss diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 3-week-old infant who underwent pyloromyotomy for pyloric stenosis 72 hours ago now has bilious nasogastric aspirates. Which of the following conditions must be urgently excluded?
questions.explanation: Bilious aspirates post-pyloromyotomy suggest distal small bowel obstruction, and malrotation with volvulus must be excluded as it is a surgical emergency. Recurrent stenosis is extremely rare and typically presents with non-bilious vomiting. Ileus is unlikely with bilious aspirates.
